title: Cycle Superhighways
problem: Car commuting from suburbs caused congestion and emissions on radial corridors into the city.
solution: A regional network of direct, wide, well-maintained cycle routes with green-wave signals, air pumps and priority at junctions, coordinated across municipalities.
outcome: Cycling on completed routes rose sharply (double-digit percentage gains) and a large share of new cyclists shifted from cars.
cost: ~1 million EUR per km on average

title: Superblocks (Superilles)
problem: High traffic volumes drove air and noise pollution and left little public space in a dense grid.
solution: Groups of nine blocks are combined so through-traffic is routed around the perimeter, freeing interior streets for pedestrians, greenery and play.
outcome: Reduced traffic and NO2 within superblocks, more public space and street life, and measurable health benefits in evaluated areas.
cost: Low-cost tactical phase scaling to full redesigns

title: Social Housing (Gemeindebau)
problem: Ensuring affordable, high-quality housing for a broad range of incomes in a growing capital.
solution: The city owns and subsidizes a large stock of municipal and cooperative housing, with quality competitions and mixed-income access rather than residual welfare housing.
outcome: Around 60% of residents live in city-owned or subsidized housing, keeping rents affordable and social mix high.
cost: Ongoing municipal investment and land banking

title: The 15-Minute City
problem: Car dependence and unequal access to daily services across neighborhoods.
solution: Planning doctrine to ensure work, shops, schools, healthcare and green space are reachable within a 15-minute walk or bike ride, paired with street reallocation.
outcome: Rapid expansion of bike lanes, school streets and local services; reduced car use in the center.
cost: Integrated into transport and planning budgets

title: District Heating with Waste Heat and Heat Pumps
problem: Heating a cold-climate city efficiently while cutting fossil fuel use.
solution: A city-wide district heating network increasingly fed by large heat pumps recovering waste heat from data centers, wastewater and seawater.
outcome: High connection rates and falling heating emissions as coal is phased out for recovered and electrified heat.

title: Amsterdam Rainproof
problem: More intense downpours overwhelm sewers and cause urban flooding.
solution: A city-wide program turning roofs, streets, gardens and squares into water-absorbing and storage surfaces via green roofs, permeable paving and water buffers.
outcome: Distributed storage capacity reduces flood risk and eases pressure on the drainage system during cloudbursts.

title: X-Road Digital Government
problem: Fragmented public services required repeated in-person paperwork.
solution: A secure data-exchange backbone (X-Road) plus digital identity lets residents access almost all government services online with a once-only data principle.
outcome: Nearly all public services are digital, saving citizens and government significant time each year.

title: Pay-As-You-Throw Waste Charging
problem: Low recycling rates and high volumes of mixed residual waste.
solution: Residents must use official charged waste bags for residual waste, while recycling and organics collection are free, pricing disposal to reward separation.
outcome: High recycling rates and reduced residual waste per capita.

title: Car-Free City Centre with Electric Shuttles
problem: Traffic dominated the historic core, harming air quality and public space.
solution: The center was closed to cars and pedestrianized, with free on-demand electric 'Kavalir' shuttles for people with limited mobility.
outcome: Revitalized public space, cleaner air, and higher pedestrian and cycling activity; recognized as a European Green Capital.

title: Vauban Car-Reduced Eco-District
problem: Building a new district without car dependence and with low energy use.
solution: A neighborhood designed around trams, cycling and walking, with reduced parking, low-energy and passive houses, and solar generation.
outcome: Very low car ownership, high share of car-free households, and low per-capita energy use.

title: Electric Vehicle Transition
problem: Transport emissions and air pollution from petrol and diesel vehicles.
solution: Incentives (toll and tax exemptions, free/discounted charging and parking, bus-lane access) plus dense public charging drove EV adoption.
outcome: Electric vehicles became the majority of new car sales, cutting local emissions and noise.
cost: Foregone tax revenue offset by charging rollout

title: Urban Green Ring and Superblocks
problem: Balancing urban growth with biodiversity and accessible green space.
solution: A continuous belt of restored parks and wetlands around the city plus interior green corridors and a mobility superblock plan.
outcome: Nearly all residents live within 300m of green space; named a European Green Capital.

title: Water Plazas for Stormwater
problem: A low-lying delta city faces flooding from heavy rainfall with limited space for storage.
solution: Public squares (e.g. Benthemplein) double as recreation space in dry weather and as temporary water-retention basins during storms.
outcome: Significant temporary storage capacity added while creating popular public space.

title: HafenCity Flood-Adapted Waterfront
problem: Redeveloping a former port on flood-prone land without conventional dikes.
solution: Buildings and infrastructure sit on raised warft platforms with flood-proof ground floors and elevated escape routes, allowing waterfront living with resilience.
outcome: A large new mixed-use district that remains functional during storm surges.

title: Geothermal District Heating
problem: Heating a cold-climate city historically reliant on imported fossil fuels.
solution: Abundant geothermal energy is tapped for a near-universal district heating network and electricity supply.
outcome: Almost all heating and electricity come from renewable geothermal and hydro, with very low heating emissions.

title: City-Centre Pedestrianization
problem: Traffic dominated the center, causing danger, pollution and low street life.
solution: Through-traffic and most parking were removed from the historic core, priority given to pedestrians, with 30 km/h limits city-wide.
outcome: Traffic deaths in the center fell to near zero, emissions dropped, and the center regained residents and commerce.

title: Compassionate Community Social Prescribing
problem: Isolation and non-medical needs drove avoidable healthcare use and poor wellbeing.
solution: Health connectors linked patients to community groups and support (social prescribing) backed by a directory of local resources.
outcome: Studies associated the program with a notable fall in emergency hospital admissions.

title: Housing First for Homelessness
problem: Long-term homelessness persisted under staircase models that required 'housing readiness'.
solution: Provide permanent housing unconditionally first, then wrap support services around tenants, converting shelters into supported flats.
outcome: Long-term homelessness fell substantially, bucking the rising trend elsewhere in Europe.
cost: Offset by reduced emergency and health costs

title: Area C Congestion Charge
problem: Heavy traffic and among the worst air quality in Europe in the dense center.
solution: A charged low-emission zone in the historic center prices entry for polluting vehicles, with revenue reinvested in transit and cycling.
outcome: Traffic entering the zone and pollutant concentrations dropped, and transit use rose.

title: City-Wide Food Waste Collection
problem: Organic waste made up a large share of landfill and a big city lacked separate food collection.
solution: Rolled out separate household food-waste collection with compostable bags and vented bins across the whole city in one program.
outcome: Became one of the largest cities globally with curbside food-waste collection, diverting large tonnages to composting and biogas.

title: Ultra Low Emission Zone
problem: Illegal levels of air pollution from road transport across the city.
solution: A daily charge on the most polluting vehicles across an expanding zone, backed by automatic number-plate enforcement and scrappage support.
outcome: Sharp falls in the number of polluting vehicles and roadside NO2 within the zone.

title: Circulation Plan
problem: Through-traffic congested the medieval center and made streets unsafe.
solution: The center was divided into sectors; drivers cannot pass directly between them and must use a ring road, while walking, cycling and transit go straight through.
outcome: Big drop in central car traffic and rise in cycling within a short period.

title: SmartSantander IoT Sensor Network
problem: Limited real-time data to manage parking, irrigation, waste and environment.
solution: Thousands of IoT sensors across the city feed an open platform and apps for parking, environmental monitoring and services.
outcome: A large-scale living lab that improved service efficiency and became a reference smart-city testbed.

title: Decidim Participatory Democracy Platform
problem: Limited transparent channels for residents to shape city decisions and budgets.
solution: An open-source digital platform for participatory budgeting, proposals and consultations, with in-person processes linked to it.
outcome: Tens of thousands of residents contributed proposals; the platform was adopted by many other cities.

title: Participatory Budgeting
problem: Residents had little direct say over capital investment priorities.
solution: A share of the city investment budget is allocated to projects proposed and voted on directly by residents each year.
outcome: Hundreds of resident-chosen projects funded, with high participation, spanning schools, greening and mobility.

title: Community Wealth Building
problem: Local economic decline and money leaking out of the local economy after failed inward investment.
solution: Anchor institutions (council, hospitals, universities) shifted procurement to local suppliers and supported cooperatives and a local bank.
outcome: A large increase in the share of spend retained locally and improved local economic indicators (the 'Preston Model').

title: Post-Industrial Regeneration
problem: Collapse of shipbuilding and steel left a polluted river and high unemployment.
solution: Coordinated riverfront cleanup, a new metro, and flagship cultural investment (the Guggenheim) anchored a services-based regeneration.
outcome: Tourism and services grew strongly and the city rebranded itself internationally (the 'Bilbao effect').

title: Cheonggyecheon Stream Restoration
problem: An elevated highway covered a former stream, adding traffic, heat and blight downtown.
solution: The highway was removed and the stream daylighted into a linear park corridor through the center.
outcome: Lower local temperatures, more biodiversity and recreation, and higher nearby land value, without the feared traffic chaos.

title: NEWater Recycled Water
problem: A dense city-state with scarce natural freshwater and import dependence.
solution: High-grade reclaimed water produced via microfiltration, reverse osmosis and UV treatment supplies industry and tops up reservoirs.
outcome: Recycled water now meets a large and growing share of national water demand, boosting water security.

title: TransMilenio Bus Rapid Transit and Ciclovia
problem: Severe congestion and limited affordable mass transit for a fast-growing city.
solution: High-capacity BRT with dedicated lanes and stations, paired with an extensive cycle network and weekly car-free 'Ciclovia' of open streets.
outcome: Millions of daily trips carried at metro-like capacity for a fraction of rail cost, plus a globally copied open-streets model.

title: Metrocable and Social Urbanism
problem: Steep informal hillside settlements were isolated, underserved and violent.
solution: Aerial cable-car lines connected hillside barrios to the metro, combined with libraries, schools and public space investment in the poorest areas ('social urbanism').
outcome: Improved access and dramatic falls in violence in connected neighborhoods, with strong social returns.

title: Climate Atlas and Ventilation Corridors
problem: A basin topography trapped heat and pollution, worsening summer heat and smog.
solution: A detailed urban climate atlas maps cold-air flows; planning rules protect ventilation corridors and hillside greenery from blocking development.
outcome: Preserved night-time cooling airflow into the city and a model for climate-informed zoning.
